author: @screen-protector
text:
For those who don't understand it too well. Everything what we produce as electricity out of a coil is AC voltage - like in your mains. This need to be rectified by diodos or a bridge rectifier.  Once done will produce a DC voltage, the one which you have in the batteries or charge your mobile devices. 

To regulate it to let's say charge your mobile, you need a voltage regulator. Least efficient are L7805 or LM7805 and they will produce 5V, but heat a lot when there's a difference in your power like up and down as well. The most efficient ones are the HF regulators (high frequency), they use what's called a PWM signal to regulate (pulse width modulation). This allows to open and close electronics switch called MOSFET to your regulated needs. And, there's a potentiometer in the regulated ones as well so that you can adjust the output voltage.

I've made videos about them in the past as well. But, the best solution for an avg. person is to build a battery bank out of old laptop batteries called 18650. These are hidden in many old laptops if you have one or can get one. You can buy a charging and protecting controller so you won't overcharge it ;). It's cheap and available on eBay, Amazon and Aliexpress just to name a few. Without it, you can overcharge those 18650s.

Now if you charge your bank out of the wind turbine, etc. You can add USB type A female socket and use it on the output regulated by that controller of 5V, or 5.1V. You can use it to charge a phone, tablet, and all other devices which have USB connection to charge them.

In series connections adds up voltage, in parallel you'll get more amps, but voltage will stay - worth remembering as well :).

author: @mancampovestiminvatam
text:
I'm a big fan of bottle dynamos as backup energy source for my bicycle lights.
BUT, charging modern phones that need 10-15 W of power for 2-3 hours makes using these dynamos completely impractical for such scenarios.
The PedalCycle, which is horribly expensive, can produce around 15 W and still not too useful for most people (syncing phone charge with commuting is difficult if not impossible).
At present, a small solar panel is my main source of energy on the bike. It delivers same wattage with almost zero drag and works even while I'm inside a building and the bike is left outside.
It charges 4 NiMH batteries configured in series. Those are enough for 1-3 hours, depending on whether there is residual light in the environment or not; and on pavement color and wetness.
Between the panel and the batteries, there is a super capacitor bank that acts as buffer both in case of solar charging and dynamo usage.
With this setup, I don't need to remember to take front lights with me, nor do I need to remember charging batteries before long rides. And, in case it discharges, I still have my belowed dynamo.
The next generation of this system will have a 10 Ah lithium power bank (cells harvested from laptop batteries) and USB-powered lights to better accomodate source voltage with light voltage.
Couple of years ago, I've been experimenting with a computer fan (converted to wind generator) as power source but failed miserably due to even lower current values. That was also intended for mostly passive harvesting.
Buuuuut, maybe this video inspires somebody to build a cheap generator that will be around the house or farm, providing light for a small room or something.
What needs to be clarified, though, is how much does a dynamo last.
